text:
Audio: And the sample size I collected, I took two students. I'm going to have high variability. I take 100 students. The variability goes down. And that's awesome. That's how we get away with bigger data sets, allow us to make more precise statements about the world. Bigger samples help us. This is why we have this hubris of big data. My data is massive. I have a good picture of the world because look, what over a billion is going to make this thing so small? But that sample has to be. I was having to be... random sample. I think it's a simple random sample in this case. with replacement. So all these rely on good sampling processes, all these results. , 2: Definition of Variance
Variance is the expected squared deviation from the expectation of X.
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
Var(X) = E (X- E[X])2
The units of the variance are the square of the units of X.
To get back to the right scale, use the standard deviation of X: SD(X) = Var(X)
Variance and SD return number, not random variables!
Describes the variability of a random variable.
Variance is the expected squared error between the random variable
and its expected value
0
Could be viewed as the chance error between a sample X and the population mean.
By Chebyshev's inequality (which you saw in Data 8, and which we won't prove here either):
.
No matter what the shape of the distribution of X is, the vast majority of the probability lies
in the interval "expectation plus or minus a few SDs."
